fields.name: termination_provisions
fields.value: Either party may terminate: (a) for convenience upon 90 days written notice; (b) immediately upon material breach that remains uncured for 30 days after written notice; (c) immediately if the other party becomes insolvent or files for bankruptcy.

fields.name: confidentiality_terms
fields.value: Each party agrees to hold in confidence all Confidential Information received from the other party. Confidential Information includes any non-public information such as business plans, financial data, customer information, technical specifications, and proprietary methodologies. This obligation survives termination for a period of three (3) years.

fields.name: intellectual_property
fields.value: All work product created by Provider specifically for Client under a SOW ('Client Work Product') is owned by Client upon full payment. Provider retains ownership of all pre-existing intellectual property, tools, methodologies, and frameworks ('Provider IP'). To the extent Provider IP is incorporated into Client Work Product, Provider grants Client a perpetual, non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use such Provider IP solely in connection with the Client Work Product.

fields.name: renewal_terms
fields.value: Automatically renews for successive one (1) year periods unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current term.

fields.name: insurance_requirements
fields.value: Provider shall maintain throughout the term: (a) Commercial General Liability insurance with limits of not less than $2,000,000 per occurrence; (b) Professional Liability / Errors & Omissions insurance with limits of not less than $5,000,000 per claim; (c) Workers' Compensation insurance as required by law; (d) Cyber Liability insurance with limits of not less than $3,000,000.

fields.name: payment_terms
fields.value: Client shall pay Provider the fees specified in each SOW. Unless otherwise stated in a SOW, invoices are due Net 30 from the invoice date. Late payments shall accrue interest at 1.5% per month.

classification.reasoning: This document is a security assessment findings report containing numbered findings with severity ratings (Critical, High, Medium, Low), supporting evidence, and remediation recommendations — all hallmark characteristics of a findings document. It also references assessment methodology, scope, standards frameworks, and includes a summary table of findings with remediation targets.

fields.name: assessment_title
fields.value: Annual Cloud Infrastructure Security Assessment

fields.name: assessor
fields.value: Pinnacle Security Advisors, LLC

fields.name: scope
fields.value: The assessment covered Meridian Financial Services' AWS cloud infrastructure across 3 production accounts, 2 staging accounts, and the centralized networking account. Specific areas included: Identity and Access Management (IAM policies, roles, MFA enforcement); Network architecture and segmentation (VPCs, security groups, NACLs); Data protection (encryption at rest and in transit, key management); Logging and monitoring (CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Config Rules); Incident response readiness; Compliance alignment with FFIEC IT Examination Handbook and NIST CSF.

fields.name: methodology
fields.value: The assessment was conducted using a combination of automated scanning (Prowler, ScoutSuite), manual configuration review, and interviews with Meridian's Cloud Engineering and InfoSec teams. Findings are rated using a four-level severity scale: Critical, High, Medium, Low.

value.title: Overly Permissive IAM Policies
value.description: 14 IAM roles across production accounts use wildcard (*) permissions for S3 and Lambda actions. Three roles have full AdministratorAccess attached, including one used by an automated deployment pipeline.
value.evidence: IAM policy analysis via Prowler identified 14 roles with overly broad permissions. The deployment pipeline role (role: cicd-deploy-prod) has AdministratorAccess with no condition keys or resource restrictions.

value.title: Unencrypted S3 Buckets Containing Customer Data
value.description: 3 of 28 production S3 buckets do not have default encryption enabled. Two of these buckets contain customer PII based on naming conventions and sample object inspection.
value.evidence: ScoutSuite scan identified buckets meridian-customer-exports, meridian-reports-archive, and meridian-temp-processing without default encryption. Object-level inspection confirmed PII presence in the first two buckets.

value.title: Incomplete CloudTrail Coverage
value.description: CloudTrail is enabled in all production accounts but data event logging is not enabled for S3 object-level operations or Lambda invocations. This limits the ability to investigate data access incidents.
value.evidence: CloudTrail configuration review confirmed that only management events are being logged. S3 data events and Lambda data events are not captured.

value.title: Security Groups Allow Unrestricted SSH Access
value.description: 7 security groups in the production VPC allow inbound SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0. While no EC2 instances currently have public IPs, this represents a latent risk if instances are later assigned public IPs or if VPC peering is modified.
value.evidence: Security group analysis identified sg-0a1b2c3d4e, sg-1f2g3h4i5j, and 5 others with inbound rule allowing TCP 22 from 0.0.0.0/0.

value.title: No Formal Incident Response Runbook for Cloud
value.description: Meridian has a general incident response plan but lacks cloud-specific runbooks for common scenarios (compromised credentials, unauthorized data access, resource hijacking).
value.evidence: Interview with InfoSec team confirmed that the existing IR plan references on-premises procedures. Cloud-specific playbooks have not been developed despite 18 months of production cloud usage.

fields.name: recommendations

value.description: Implement least-privilege policies for all production roles. Replace AdministratorAccess on the CI/CD role with scoped permissions limited to the specific services and resources required for deployment.
Enable SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS default encryption on all production buckets immediately. Implement an SCP (Service Control Policy) to prevent creation of unencrypted buckets.

Enable S3 data event logging for all buckets containing sensitive data. Enable Lambda data event logging for production functions. Estimated additional cost: ~$200/month based on current event volume.
Restrict SSH access to specific CIDR ranges (corporate VPN and bastion host IPs only). Implement AWS Systems Manager Session Manager as the primary remote access method.
Develop cloud-specific IR runbooks for the top 5 incident scenarios. Conduct a tabletop exercise within 60 days.

fields.source_location: Individual finding 'Recommendation' subsections and 'Recommendations Summary' table

fields.name: executive_summary
fields.value: Pinnacle Security Advisors conducted a comprehensive security assessment of Meridian Financial Services' AWS cloud infrastructure during the period April 15–May 10, 2024. The assessment covered 3 production accounts, 2 staging accounts, and the centralized networking account. Overall, Meridian's cloud security posture is rated Moderate Risk — foundational controls are in place, but several gaps in IAM governance, network segmentation, and data encryption practices require remediation before the upcoming regulatory examination.

fields.name: risk_rating
fields.value: Moderate Risk — Foundational security controls are in place (GuardDuty active, Config Rules deployed, MFA enforced for console access), but the combination of overly permissive IAM policies and unencrypted customer data buckets presents material risk that should be addressed prior to the Q3 regulatory examination.

fields.name: standards_referenced
value: FFIEC IT Examination Handbook — Information Security
NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) v2.0
CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark v3.0
AWS Well-Architected Framework — Security Pillar

classification.reasoning: This document explicitly defines project phases with date ranges, a team roles table with allocations, a milestone table with gate criteria, dependencies, and a risk register — all hallmarks of a project plan. It provides a detailed work breakdown structure for a data migration engagement across four sequential phases with specific owners and durations.

fields.name: project_name
fields.value: Data Platform Modernization
fields.source_location: Document title and 'Project Name' header field at top of document

fields.name: project_manager
fields.value: Marcus Chen
fields.source_location: 'Project Manager' header field at top of document

fields.name: start_date
fields.source_location: 'Start Date' header field at top of document

fields.name: target_end_date
fields.source_location: 'Target End Date' header field at top of document

value.name: Phase 1: Assessment
key_activities: Inventory all Oracle schemas, tables, views, and stored procedures
Document existing ETL pipelines (Informatica mappings, schedules, dependencies)
Catalog downstream consumers (BI reports, data feeds, APIs)
Assess data quality and identify transformation requirements
Produce Current State Assessment Report

value.name: Phase 2: Architecture Design
key_activities: Design target state architecture (Redshift, Glue, S3, Lake Formation)
Define data model mapping from Oracle to Redshift
Design ETL pipeline architecture in AWS Glue
Define security model (IAM, encryption, VPC configuration)
Produce Target State Architecture Document
Architecture review with Client technical leadership

value.name: Phase 3: Migration Execution
key_activities: Set up AWS infrastructure (Redshift cluster, Glue jobs, S3 buckets)
Migrate schemas in priority order: Finance → Risk → Operations → HR → Compliance
Rewrite 12 ETL pipelines from Informatica to AWS Glue
Unit test each migrated schema and pipeline
Produce Migration Runbook and Rollback Plan

value.name: Phase 4: Validation and Transition
key_activities: Execute parallel run (30 business days)
Compare Oracle and Redshift outputs for all critical reports
Conduct 4 knowledge transfer sessions with Client data engineering team
Resolve any discrepancies identified during parallel run
Produce Parallel Run Validation Report
Obtain Client sign-off

value.name: Architecture Approved
value.gate_criteria: Architecture document approved by Client CTO

value.name: Parallel Run Start
value.gate_criteria: All migration artifacts deployed to production

value.name: Final Sign-off
value.gate_criteria: Parallel run report accepted, knowledge transfer complete

fields.name: dependencies
value: Client DBA team availability for schema documentation (8 hours/week minimum)
AWS account provisioning and IAM role creation by Client Cloud team
VPN access for Provider team members
Source system freeze windows coordinated with business owners
Client InfoSec approval of security architecture before Phase 3

value.risk: Undocumented Oracle stored procedures delay assessment
value.mitigation: Allocate additional discovery time; engage Oracle DBA contractors if needed
Architecture phase includes complexity scoring; will flag if >20% over estimate
Build in 2-week buffer before final sign-off; define acceptable variance thresholds upfront
Secure named Client resources at SOW signing; escalation path to Engagement Lead

fields.name: success_criteria
value: All 47 production schemas migrated with zero data loss
All 12 ETL pipelines operational in AWS Glue with performance within 20% of Oracle baseline
Parallel run demonstrates <0.01% variance in critical report outputs
Client data engineering team rated 'confident' in post-KT survey (≥4/5 average)
Project completed within budget ($400,000) and timeline (August 31, 2024)

fields.name: communication_plan
fields.value: Weekly Status Report: Every Friday, distributed to Steering Committee. Bi-weekly Steering Committee: Every other Wednesday, 60 minutes. Daily Standup (during Phase 3): 15 minutes, Provider team + Client DBA lead. Ad-hoc Escalation: Via Teams channel #data-migration, response within 4 hours.

classification.reasoning: This document explicitly describes the system components, technology stack, integration patterns, security considerations, and scalability approach for a cloud data platform, all hallmark characteristics of an architecture document. It also includes design decision rationale (ADR-style table) and addresses non-functional requirements such as security, scalability, and compliance constraints.

fields.name: system_name
fields.value: Meridian Cloud Data Platform (CDP)

fields.name: author
fields.value: Rachel Kim, Cloud Architect

value.name: Data Ingestion Layer
value.responsibility: Ingest data from upstream source systems via batch ETL and near-real-time streaming.
details: AWS Glue crawlers discover and catalog source data
Glue ETL jobs handle transformation and loading
S3 serves as the landing zone and staging area
Supports CSV, Parquet, JSON, and AVRO formats

value.name: Data Storage Layer
value.responsibility: Store structured and semi-structured data for analytics consumption.
details: Redshift cluster: 4x ra3.4xlarge nodes (production), 2x ra3.xlplus (staging)
Redshift Spectrum for querying data directly in S3
Data lake on S3 for raw and archived data (Parquet format, partitioned by date)
Lifecycle policies: hot data in Redshift (2 years), warm data in S3 Standard (5 years), cold data in S3 Glacier (7+ years)

value.name: Data Catalog and Governance
value.responsibility: Centralized metadata management, access control, and data lineage.
details: Lake Formation manages fine-grained access control (column-level security)
Glue Data Catalog provides schema registry and searchable metadata
Data classification tags applied to PII and sensitive financial data

value.name: Analytics and Reporting
value.responsibility: Serve analytics consumers with dashboards and ad-hoc query capabilities.
details: QuickSight dashboards for executive and operational reporting
JDBC/ODBC endpoints for existing BI tools (Tableau, Power BI)
Redshift query editor for analyst self-service

value.name: Orchestration
value.responsibility: Coordinate ETL workflows, manage dependencies, handle retries and error states.
details: Step Functions state machines for complex multi-step ETL workflows
EventBridge schedules for time-based triggers (daily, hourly)
SNS notifications for pipeline failures and SLA breaches

value.name: Monitoring and Observability
value.responsibility: Monitor platform health, query performance, and security events.
details: CloudWatch dashboards for Redshift performance (query latency, disk usage, connection count)
CloudWatch Alarms for resource utilization thresholds
CloudTrail for audit logging of all API calls and data access

fields.name: technology_stack
value: AWS Redshift (RA3) — primary data warehouse
AWS Glue — ETL and data cataloging
Amazon S3 — data lake and staging
AWS Lake Formation — governance and access control
AWS Step Functions — workflow orchestration
Amazon EventBridge — event-driven scheduling
Amazon QuickSight — business intelligence
Amazon CloudWatch — monitoring and alerting
AWS CloudTrail — audit logging
AWS KMS — encryption key management
AWS IAM — identity and access management
Terraform — infrastructure as code

fields.name: deployment_model
fields.value: Cloud-native on AWS, single region (us-east-1), multi-AZ for high availability. All infrastructure provisioned and managed via Terraform with state stored in S3 and locked via DynamoDB. CI/CD pipeline via AWS CodePipeline deploying Glue jobs and Redshift DDL changes.

fields.name: security_considerations
value: All data encrypted at rest using AWS KMS (customer-managed keys)
All data encrypted in transit via TLS 1.2+
VPC with private subnets only — no public endpoints
Lake Formation column-level access control for PII fields
MFA required for all console access
IAM roles follow least-privilege principle (per-service, per-environment)
Quarterly access reviews coordinated with InfoSec
CloudTrail logs shipped to centralized security account

fields.name: scalability_approach
fields.value: Redshift RA3 nodes with managed storage provide elastic scaling — compute and storage scale independently. Concurrency Scaling automatically adds cluster capacity during peak query periods. For data volume growth beyond 5 years, transition older data to Redshift Spectrum (query S3 directly) to maintain query performance on hot data.

value.rationale: Client has existing AWS Enterprise Agreement; Redshift integrates natively with Lake Formation and existing IAM model
value.alternatives_considered: Snowflake (better concurrency, higher cost), BigQuery (wrong cloud)

value.rationale: Serverless, no infrastructure to manage, native integration with Glue Data Catalog
value.alternatives_considered: MWAA (Airflow on AWS), Step Functions only, Informatica Cloud

value.rationale: Managed storage allows compute and storage to scale independently; RA3 pricing more favorable for 2TB+ datasets
value.alternatives_considered: DC2 (faster local SSD, but storage-coupled scaling)

value.rationale: Team expertise, multi-cloud portability if needed, better module ecosystem

fields.name: constraints
value: Must remain within AWS due to existing Enterprise Agreement (no multi-cloud)
All data must reside in us-east-1 per regulatory requirements
Maximum acceptable query latency for executive dashboards: 10 seconds
Platform must support SOC 2 Type II and FFIEC audit requirements
Total infrastructure cost target: ≤$15,000/month (excluding data transfer)

findings.title: Assessment Complete Milestone Date Conflict: SOW vs. Project Plan
findings.description: The SOW specifies the 'Assessment Complete' milestone target date as April 15, 2024, while the project plan lists the same milestone as April 12, 2024. This 3-day discrepancy affects payment trigger timing ($50,000 milestone payment) and contractual obligations.

field_references.value: {"name": "Assessment Complete", "target_date": "2024-04-15", "payment": "$50,000"}
{"name": "Assessment Complete", "target_date": "2024-04-12", "gate_criteria": "Assessment report delivered and accepted"}

findings.title: Termination Notice Period Conflict: SOW (30 Days) vs. MSA (90 Days)
findings.description: The SOW specifies a 30-day written notice period for termination for convenience, while the Master Services Agreement specifies 90 days written notice for termination for convenience. These documents govern the same engagement, and the SOW's shorter notice period likely conflicts with or supersedes the MSA without an explicit priority-of-documents clause noted in the extractions. This creates legal ambiguity about which termination period controls.

field_references.value: Either party may terminate this SOW with 30 days written notice.
Either party may terminate: (a) for convenience upon 90 days written notice...

findings.title: Migration Target Platform Conflict: Status Report (Redshift) vs. SOW (AWS Generic)
findings.description: The SOW references 'Migrated schemas and ETL pipelines (in AWS)' without specifying Redshift. The status report confirms migration to Redshift ('Completed migration of 38 of 47 production schemas to Redshift'). The architecture document specifies AWS Redshift (RA3) as the primary data warehouse. While the architecture document resolves the platform choice, the SOW's deliverable language is ambiguous and does not contractually lock in Redshift, creating a potential scope dispute if the client later objects to the platform selection.

field_references.value: Migrated schemas and ETL pipelines (in AWS)
Completed migration of 38 of 47 production schemas to Redshift (81% complete)
AWS Redshift (RA3) — primary data warehouse

findings.title: Architecture Approved Milestone Date Conflict: SOW vs. Project Plan
findings.description: The SOW milestone list includes 'Architecture Approved' but the extracted SOW milestone data is truncated. The project plan lists 'Architecture Approved' with a target date of May 17, 2024. The status report confirms this milestone as 'Complete.' While the milestone appears consistent between the project plan and status report, the SOW's truncated milestones field prevents full verification of the SOW target date for this milestone.

field_references.value: {"name": "Architecture Approved", "target_date": "[truncated]", "payment": "[truncated]"}
{"name": "Architecture Approved", "target_date": "2024-05-17"}

findings.title: Archived Data Inclusion Conflict: SOW Out-of-Scope vs. Status Report Decision Request
findings.description: The SOW explicitly lists 'Migration of non-production or archived data' as out-of-scope. However, the status report raises a decision request asking the client to 'Confirm whether archived data (pre-2020) should be included in parallel run validation.' Executing archived data migration or validation without a formal SOW amendment would constitute unauthorized scope creep with potential cost and contractual implications.

field_references.value: Migration of non-production or archived data
Confirm whether archived data (pre-2020) should be included in parallel run validation

findings.title: Project Start Date Conflict: SOW Effective Date vs. Project Plan Start Date
findings.description: The SOW lists an effective date of March 1, 2024, which aligns with the 'SOW Execution' milestone. However, the project plan lists a start date of March 4, 2024 (Monday following effective date). While this 3-day gap may reflect a practical workday start, it is not documented as an assumption in either document and could affect SLA or milestone calculations measured from the effective date.

findings.title: Security Assessment Findings Not Reflected in Architecture Document or Project Risks
findings.description: The security assessment (conducted April 15–May 10, 2024) identified critical findings including overly permissive IAM policies (14 roles with wildcard permissions, 3 with AdministratorAccess) and unencrypted EBS volumes. The architecture document lists security controls including KMS encryption and VPC private subnets but does not reference or address the specific critical findings from the Pinnacle Security Advisors assessment. The project plan risks and the status report risks and blockers also make no mention of these security findings, despite the assessment predating the status report (June 10–14, 2024). This represents a gap in risk tracking and remediation accountability.

field_references.value: {"title": "Overly Permissive IAM Policies", "description": "14 IAM roles across production accounts use wildcard (*) permissions..."}
Moderate Risk — combination of overly permissive IAM policies and unencrypted EBS volumes
All data encrypted at rest using AWS KMS (customer-managed keys); VPC with private subnets only
[No reference to IAM or EBS security findings from Pinnacle assessment]

findings.title: IAM Role Provisioning Assumption in SOW Not Cross-Referenced Against Security Assessment Critical Finding
findings.description: The SOW assumption states 'AWS accounts and IAM roles will be provisioned [by the Client Cloud team].' The security assessment found that 3 IAM roles have full AdministratorAccess, which the assessment flags as Critical priority for remediation. If Apex Consulting's team members are operating under or alongside these over-permissive IAM roles during the migration, this poses a material security risk that is not flagged in the project plan, SOW assumptions, or status report.

(truncated, presumably 'Knowledge Transfer' documentation), but the project plan phases and the status report do not provide enough detail to confirm whether this KT program is formally scoped and whether prior sessions (1 and 2) were delivered. findings.title: PII Masking Requirement Not Present in SOW Assumptions, Architecture Document, or Project Plan Risks
findings.description: The status report identifies PII masking for the HR schema as a High severity open risk and a current blocker, with a mitigation note indicating masking rules have not yet been provided by Client InfoSec. Neither the SOW assumptions, the project plan risks, nor the architecture document references a PII masking requirement or data classification framework for HR domain data. The architecture document mentions 'Lake Formation column-level security' but does not specifically address PII masking. This gap suggests the PII requirement emerged after project initiation without being formally incorporated into project documentation.

findings.title: Compliance Domain Source System Access Blocker Not Reflected in Project Plan Dependencies
findings.description: The status report identifies a current blocker: 'Access to Compliance domain source system pending security review (submitted May 28).' The project plan lists source system access as a dependency, but does not specifically enumerate Compliance domain source system access as a discrete dependency item or risk. Given that Compliance schema migration is part of the remaining 9 schemas, this unresolved access issue threatens the project end date of August 31, 2024.

findings.title: Security Assessment Findings Not Triggering MSA Compliance or Insurance Obligations
findings.description: The MSA specifies insurance requirements including Professional Liability/E&O coverage, and the SOW governs the migration work on the AWS environment. The security assessment found critical vulnerabilities (unencrypted EBS volumes, overly permissive IAM) in the environment where active migration work is occurring. Neither the status report nor any project document references whether these findings trigger any notification, remediation, or liability obligations under the MSA. The MSA's confidentiality terms and liability cap ($1,000,000) may be relevant if a breach occurs in this environment during migration.

findings.title: End-User Training Explicitly Out of Scope in SOW but Knowledge Transfer Sessions Are Occurring
findings.description: The SOW explicitly lists 'End-user training on BI tools' as out of scope. The status report references ongoing Knowledge Transfer sessions (Session 3 upcoming). While Knowledge Transfer to technical staff (e.g., CloudWatch monitoring) may be distinguishable from BI tool end-user training, the boundary is not formally documented. If any KT session covers BI tool usage, it would constitute out-of-scope work without a change order.

cross_document_analysis.summary: The document set is largely aligned on core identifiers (project name, parties, total budget, end date, and schema count), with strong cross-document consistency across the SOW, project plan, and status report for operational tracking. However, three high-severity issues require immediate attention: a contractual conflict between the SOW's 30-day and the MSA's 90-day termination notice periods; a 3-day discrepancy in the Assessment Complete milestone date between the SOW ($50,000 payment trigger) and the project plan; and a critical gap whereby the security assessment's findings (overly permissive IAM policies, unencrypted EBS volumes) have not been incorporated into the project risk register, status report, or architecture document despite being identified before the June status report period. Additionally, two emerging scope risks — the PII masking requirement for the HR schema and the client's request to include archived data (explicitly out of scope in the SOW) — require formal change order evaluation to prevent unauthorized scope creep and protect both parties' contractual positions.

narrative_summary:
## Document Analysis Assessment Summary

Six project documents were analyzed for the Apex Consulting client engagement: a Statement of Work, Master Services Agreement, status report, security findings report, project plan, and architecture document. All six documents were classified with high confidence (98–99%) and achieved complete field extraction, with no validation failures across 70 total extracted fields. Data quality is strong, providing a reliable foundation for cross-document analysis.

Despite clean extraction results, the analysis surfaced nine findings of High or Medium severity representing meaningful contractual, security, and scope risks. Most critically, a direct conflict exists between the SOW's 30-day termination notice period and the MSA's 90-day requirement — a legal ambiguity that must be resolved before either party can rely on either provision. Separately, the "Assessment Complete" milestone is dated April 15, 2024 in the SOW and April 12, 2024 in the project plan; given that this milestone triggers a $50,000 payment, the 3-day discrepancy requires immediate reconciliation. The most operationally urgent finding is that critical security vulnerabilities identified by Pinnacle Security Advisors — including 14 IAM roles with wildcard permissions, three with full AdministratorAccess, and unencrypted EBS volumes — appear nowhere in the project risk register, status report, or architecture document, despite the assessment predating the June status period. Active migration work is occurring in this environment, and the SOW assumption that the client's cloud team would provision IAM roles does not account for these findings.

Two emerging scope risks also require immediate attention. The status report's decision request asking whether archived data (pre-2020) should be included in parallel run validation directly contradicts the SOW's explicit out-of-scope exclusion of non-production and archived data. Additionally, a PII masking requirement for the HR schema — currently a High severity blocker — does not appear in the SOW, project plan, or architecture document, suggesting the requirement surfaced post-initiation without formal documentation.

**Recommended next steps are as follows:** (1) Engage legal counsel to resolve the SOW/MSA termination notice conflict and establish a clear priority-of-documents clause; (2) issue a contract amendment to align the Assessment Complete milestone date and protect the $50,000 payment trigger; (3) formally incorporate the Pinnacle Security Advisors findings into the project risk register and assign remediation owners with target dates; (4) evaluate the archived data and PII masking items for change order treatment before any related work proceeds; and (5) obtain untruncated SOW deliverables and milestone fields to close the remaining verification gaps.
